name: Tarot de Marseille — Nicolas Conver 1760 (The Printed Standard)
description:
# Tarot de Marseille (Conver 1760)

> **Reading the citations.** Each substantive claim carries a bracketed key, e.g.
> `[Dummett1980]`. Keys resolve in **References** at the foot of the file, grouped by
> tier: **[P]** primary sources (the period texts and the decks themselves), **[S]**
> secondary scholarship that works *through* primary documents (prioritised), and **[T]**
> tertiary web/collector pages (used only for uncontroversial detail). Where a date is
> itself contested, the claim says so rather than citing a single page as settled.

## At a glance

The **canonical printed standard** of Western tarot — woodblock-and-stencil cards whose
22-trump sequence (C-order) became *the* reference layout `[Dummett1980] [Depaulis1984]`.
Nicolas Conver's 1760 Marseille edition is the most renowned single witness
`[Conver1760-obj] [Depaulis1984]`. Born a **game** deck, it is the exact pack the
18th-century occultists picked up and re-read as an Egyptian oracle `[CdG1781] [DDD1996]`.

## Origin & dating

The "Marseille" pattern stabilised in 17th–18th-century French and Swiss card-making
`[Dummett1980] [Depaulis1984]`. **Nicolas Conver** signed and printed his Marseille deck in
**1760** from engraved woodblocks, coloured by stencil `[Conver1760-obj] [Depaulis1984]`.
It was not the first Marseille-pattern deck — it follows earlier Marseille cardmakers such
as **Chosson** (the well-known Chosson deck bears the date **1736**, though that date is
itself debated in the scholarship) `[Depaulis1984] [DDD1996]`. The Conver woodblocks were
reused for over a century, and the pattern was later re-standardised for the modern era by
**Paul Marteau's** Grimaud *Ancien Tarot de Marseille* (c.1930), the prototype for most
20th-century French decks `[Marteau1949] [Depaulis1984]`.

## Provenance & evidence

Surviving Conver decks are held in public collections (e.g. the **Bibliothèque nationale
de France** and the **British Museum**, which hold Marseille-pattern cards and sheets), and
the Conver woodblocks are claimed by the successor **Camoin** firm `[BnF-obj] [BM-obj]
[Camoin-T]`. The lineage **Chosson (1736?) → Conver (1760) → Grimaud/Marteau (c.1930)** is
documented in the standard catalogues `[Depaulis1984] [Dummett1980]`.

## Game or divination?

**Game first.** The Tarot de Marseille was printed and sold for the trick-taking game
*tarot* — a living game still played in France — and the documentary record treats it as a
game pack for its entire early life `[DummettMcLeod2004] [Dummett1980]`. **But** this is the
hinge deck: because the Marseille was the common, available pattern in 1780s Paris, it is
the deck **Court de Gébelin** allegorised as an Egyptian "Book of Thoth" in 1781, and the
one **Etteilla** and his followers adapted into a divination system from the later 1780s
`[CdG1781] [Ett1785] [DDD1996]`. So the *object* is a game deck and the *occult tradition*
is a later use grafted onto it; on the documentary evidence, no divinatory reading of these
trumps is attested before 1781 `[Dummett1980] [DDD1996]`. **The grammar should label the
deck "game," and the divinatory meanings as a documented later overlay, not an origin**
`[Dummett1980] [DDD1996]`.

## The fear question

**No fear as a game** — the Tarot de Marseille was an ordinary, regulated commercial
product of the French card trade, subject to the state playing-card tax and its control
stamp `[Depaulis1984]`. The instructive twist comes later: by the 19th century the *same
physical pattern* could be innocent leisure in one hand and, in another, the working tool
of a prosecuted fortune-teller — prosecution that ran on vagrancy and anti-fraud law, not
on anything about the cards themselves (see the fear-question discussion and statutes cited
in `00-overview-history-of-tarot.mdx`) `[Dummett1980]`. The deck did not change; the **use
and the user's social position** did.

## Counter-voices

From **Éliphas Lévi** onward, esoteric writers treated the Marseille's "purity" as evidence
of preserved ancient (Egyptian/Kabbalistic) symbolism `[Lévi1856] [DDD1996]`. The
document-first reading is that the imagery is a worn, simplified descendant of the Italian
trionfi, and that its apparent "perfection" is **printing-tradition conservatism** — block
copied from block — rather than transmitted doctrine `[Dummett1980]`. That said, the
Marseille's role as the **canvas onto which modern esoteric tarot was built** is real and
historically central: it is the genuine bridge object between game and oracle `[DDD1996]`.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Distinguish in the grammar between the *Marseille pattern* (broad type) and the *Conver
  1760 edition* (specific witness) `[Depaulis1984]`.
- Treat the **Chosson "1736"** date as contested, not settled `[Depaulis1984] [DDD1996]`.
- Note the Besançon/Swiss **1JJ** variant (Jupiter/Juno for Pope/Popess) — see
  `15-besancon-1jj.mdx`.

## References

**[P] Primary sources** (period texts and objects)

- `[CdG1781]` Antoine Court de Gébelin, *Le Monde primitif analysé et comparé avec le monde
  moderne*, vol. VIII (Paris, 1781) — the essay declaring the tarot an Egyptian Book of
  Thoth; describes the very Marseille-type cards he saw in play. *(public domain)*
- `[Ett1785]` Etteilla (Jean-Baptiste Alliette), *Manière de se récréer avec le jeu de
  cartes nommées tarots* (Paris, 1783–1785) — the first tarot-divination method-books.
  *(public domain)*
- `[Lévi1856]` Éliphas Lévi, *Dogme et rituel de la haute magie* (Paris, 1854–1856) — the
  19th-century source of the tarot–Kabbalah "ancient symbolism" claim. *(public domain)*
- `[Marteau1949]` Paul Marteau, *Le Tarot de Marseille* (Paris: Grimaud, 1949) — the
  standardiser's own account of the Grimaud pattern.
- `[Conver1760-obj]` The Nicolas Conver Tarot de Marseille, Marseille, 1760 — the deck
  itself (signed/dated exemplars in public collections).
- `[BnF-obj]` Bibliothèque nationale de France, Département des Estampes — Marseille-pattern
  tarot holdings (primary objects).
- `[BM-obj]` British Museum — Marseille-pattern playing-card sheets and cards (primary
  objects; see collection online).

**[S] Secondary scholarship that reads the primaries** (prioritised)

- `[Dummett1980]` Michael Dummett, *The Game of Tarot: From Ferrara to Salt Lake City*
  (London: Duckworth, 1980) — the foundational document-based history; establishes the
  game-first chronology and the A/B/C trump orders.
- `[DDD1996]` Ronald Decker, Thierry Depaulis & Michael Dummett, *A Wicked Pack of Cards:
  The Origins of the Occult Tarot* (London: Duckworth, 1996) — traces the 1781→19th-c.
  occult overlay through the primary French sources (de Gébelin, Etteilla, Lévi).
- `[Depaulis1984]` Thierry Depaulis, *Tarot, jeu et magie* (Paris: Bibliothèque nationale,
  1984) — exhibition catalogue grounded directly in the BnF's primary holdings (Marseille
  cardmakers, Conver, the tax stamps).
- `[DummettMcLeod2004]` Michael Dummett & John McLeod, *A History of Games Played with the
  Tarot Pack* (Lewiston: Edwin Mellen Press, 2004) — the definitive account of tarot as a
  *game*, including the French game still played today.

**[T] Tertiary** (web/collector pages — uncontroversial detail only)

- `[Camoin-T]` en.camoin.com, "The 1760 Edition of Nicolas Conver" — the Camoin–Conver
  woodblock claim and reproduction notes.
- `[TarotHeritage-T]` tarot-heritage.com, "1760 Nicolas Conver Tarot de Marseille Restored."

> **Provenance honesty:** work-level citations. Page/folio numbers are not given unless
> independently verified; where a claim rests on a contested date (Chosson 1736), the text
> flags the dispute rather than implying a settled source.

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13).** The elemental suit labels (Coins=Earth, Cups=Water, Swords=Air, Batons=Fire) are a later **Golden Dawn overlay**, not native to a 1760 game pack — read them as a modern reading aid, not the deck's own scheme. The 1760 block date sits oddly with the BnF authority record for Nicolas Conver (1784–1833); the 1760 cutting is debated. See [research/decks/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/decks/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).

sections.Iconography: A traveller in motley walks rightward with a staff and bundle, a small animal tearing at his trailing leg. He alone carries no number — outside the count.
sections.Upright: Beginnings, wandering, freedom, the leap taken without calculation; the unattached spirit moving on.
sections.Reversed: Folly, heedlessness, dispersion; movement without direction or commitment.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
A traveller in motley walks rightward with a staff over his shoulder and a bundle, while a small animal (a dog or cat) tears at his trailing leg, baring his thigh. He alone carries **no number** — outside the running count of the trumps. *Le Mat* means "the madman" [@web_fool_wp; @dummett1980]. 

*Sources:* [@decker1996], [@dummett1980], [@web_fool_wp].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).
sections.Scene: Le Mat (The Fool) in the Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille shows a wandering figure in a motley costume — a parti-colored garment of patchwork suggesting the jester or vagrant — walking toward the right edge of the card, his bindle stick over his shoulder. A small dog or animal nips at his leg or backside, tearing at his garment. The Mat is unnumbered (though later prints sometimes number him 0) and is traditionally placed either before or after the trump sequence. Conver's Mat shows the characteristic blue-and-yellow Marseille colorwork, the figure's torn stocking revealing a wound or bare leg, the animal's aggression indifferent to the Mat's cheerful or oblivious stride.
sections.Symbol: The Fool walking with bindle, animal nipping — the unnumbered wanderer outside the trump sequence, indifference to the world's opinion as the posture of divine folly.

sections.Iconography: A young mountebank stands at a table of cups, coins, and knives, a wand in one hand, his hat brim a lemniscate. The lowest trump, the everyman entertainer.
sections.Upright: Skill, initiative, the will gathering its tools, a beginning in the maker's hands.
sections.Reversed: Trickery, false starts, cleverness without substance, manipulation.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
A young mountebank stands behind a table strewn with cups, coins, and knives, a wand in one hand and a small ball in the other, his wide hat-brim drawn as a lazy figure of eight. The lowest numbered trump — the fairground conjuror / *bateleur* ("sleight-of-hand artist") [@web_magician_wp; @dummett1980]. 

*Sources:* [@decker1996], [@dummett1980], [@web_magician_wp].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).
sections.Scene: Le Bateleur (The Juggler/Magician) stands at a table set with his implements — cups, balls, a knife, and other small tools of the street conjurer's trade. His wide-brimmed hat curves up at the sides (or has the lemniscate-like shape that appears in some versions), his coat and breeches in Marseille colors. With his right hand he holds or gestures with a wand, while the left indicates the table. The Bateleur is the showman and sleight-of-hand artist, a figure from popular street performance rather than the learned magician of later occult tarot. In the Conver 1760 woodblock printing, the details of his table and implements are rendered with the characteristic bold lines and primary colors of the Marseille engraving tradition.
sections.Symbol: The Juggler at his table with wand — the showman of street performance, skill and dexterity as the manipulation of surface and appearance.

sections.Iconography: A seated, veiled woman in papal tiara holds an open book, a curtain behind her.
sections.Upright: Hidden knowledge, intuition, patience, the wisdom that waits and conceals.
sections.Reversed: Secrets misused, surface knowledge, withdrawal, sterile silence.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
A seated, veiled woman wearing the **papal tiara** holds an open book on her lap, a curtain or veil behind her. A frankly heterodox image — a female pope [@dummett1980; @tarot_heritage]. 

*Sources:* [@decker1996], [@dummett1980].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).
sections.Scene: La Papesse (The Popess) is a seated female figure wearing the papal tiara and holding an open book on her lap. She is dressed in the blue and white robes of a female pope — a figure whose iconographic origins have been debated (Pope Joan legend, the Guglielmites, the Visconti allusion to a female figurehead). In the Conver 1760, she is a serene authority figure behind a veil or curtain, the open book on her lap suggesting secret or sacred knowledge withheld from view. The tiara and robes make her an exact feminine counterpart of Le Pape (V), holding spiritual authority in its feminine form.
sections.Symbol: The Popess with open book and tiara — female spiritual authority seated in the tradition's forbidden space, knowledge as the book held in her lap.

sections.Iconography: A crowned woman enthroned with sceptre and an eagle shield.
sections.Upright: Abundance, fertility, creativity, worldly feminine authority and the flowering of forms.
sections.Reversed: Vanity, sterility, smothering, creativity blocked or squandered.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
A crowned woman enthroned, holding a **sceptre** and a **shield bearing an eagle** (the heraldic imperial eagle, carried down from the Italian *trionfi*). She is numbered **III** [@web_empress_wp; @tarot_heritage]. 

*Sources:* [@decker1996], [@tarot_heritage], [@web_empress_wp].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).
sections.Scene: L'Impératrice (The Empress) is a seated female figure bearing the orb and scepter of imperial sovereignty, or sometimes a shield with an eagle. She is crowned with the imperial crown, her posture one of relaxed authority. In the Conver 1760, the Empress is a robust figure of earthly female power — less ethereal than the Popess, more actively governing. Her right hand may hold a scepter while her left bears the orb, or she holds a shield. The characteristic Marseille color palette renders her robes in bold reds, yellows, and blues.
sections.Symbol: The Empress with orb and scepter or eagle shield — earthly female sovereignty, the active governance of the material and natural world.

sections.Iconography: A bearded sovereign sits in profile, legs crossed in the figure of four, sceptre and eagle shield at hand.
sections.Upright: Authority, structure, stability, reason giving the world its order.
sections.Reversed: Domination, rigidity, tyranny, order hardened into oppression.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
A bearded sovereign seated in profile, one leg crossed over the other in the **figure of four**, sceptre in hand and the **eagle shield** beside him. Numbered with the additive **IIII** [@web_marseille_wp; @dummett1980]. 

*Sources:* [@decker1996], [@dummett1980], [@web_marseille_wp].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).
sections.Scene: L'Empereur (The Emperor) is a crowned male figure seated on a throne, his body often turned three-quarters or in profile to show his eagle-emblazoned armor or robes. He holds the scepter of temporal authority, and his posture is one of settled, secure power. In the Conver 1760, the Emperor is rendered in the woodblock style with his crown and imperial regalia, the characteristic Marseille coloring giving his armor and throne the colors of earthly dominion. The throne may show decorative elements or animal heads.
sections.Symbol: The Emperor enthroned with scepter — temporal masculine authority in its settled, governing form, the secular counterpart of the Pape.

sections.Iconography: A tiara'd pope raises his hand in blessing over two tonsured clerics, a pillar at each side.
sections.Upright: Teaching, tradition, blessing, the bridge between the human and the sacred.
sections.Reversed: Dogma, empty orthodoxy, conformity, the letter without the spirit.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
A tiara'd pope raises his right hand in blessing over **two tonsured clerics** seen from behind, a pillar at either side, a triple cross / staff in his hand [@dummett1980; @tarot_heritage]. 

*Sources:* [@decker1996], [@dummett1980].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).
sections.Scene: Le Pape (The Pope) is an enthroned figure in papal vestments — the triple tiara, the red and white robes of the papacy — with his right hand raised in the blessing gesture and his left holding a triple cross or scepter. Before him kneel two smaller figures (clergy or petitioners), receiving his blessing or instruction. The Pape is the spiritual authority in its male hierarchical form, the institutional head of the sacred order. In the Conver 1760, his tiara and vestments are rendered in the primary colors of the Marseille tradition.
sections.Symbol: The Pope blessing two kneeling clergy — male ecclesiastical authority, the formal transmission of sacred order from the institutional center.

sections.Iconography: A youth stands between two women under a blindfolded Cupid drawing his bow — the choice between two ways.
sections.Upright: Love, union, and above all a choice that must be made; the heart at a crossroads.
sections.Reversed: Indecision, temptation, a union founded on weakness, choice deferred.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
A youth stands **between two women**, under a **blindfolded Cupid** in a sunburst drawing his bow — the image of a choice between two ways (often read as virtue vs. vice) [@web_marseille_wp; @dummett1980]. 

*Sources:* [@decker1996], [@dummett1980], [@web_marseille_wp].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).
sections.Scene: L'Amoureux (The Lovers) in the Conver 1760 shows three figures: a young man at the center stands between two women (one on each side), while above him a winged Cupid draws his bow. This is the traditional Marseille Amoureux — not yet the paired lovers of the Waite-Smith but the scene of a young man's choice between two women, the arrow of love about to strike. The three figures are rendered in the bright woodblock colors of the Marseille tradition, the young man's posture suggesting hesitation or attraction toward one or the other.
sections.Symbol: The Young Man between two women, Cupid above — the moment of erotic and social choice, love as the arrow that resolves the indecision of youth.

sections.Iconography: A crowned prince stands in a canopied chariot drawn by two horses facing different ways; he holds no reins.
sections.Upright: Victory, drive, mastery, holding opposing forces together and advancing by will.
sections.Reversed: Loss of control, conflict, a triumph undone by pride or haste.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
A crowned prince stands in a **canopied chariot drawn by two horses** facing slightly different ways; he holds **no reins**. The Conver block carries the engraver's initials **"V T" on the chariot's shield** — a key clue in the dating puzzle [@dummett1980; @web_vs_conver_heritage]. 

*Sources:* [@decker1996], [@dummett1980], [@web_vs_conver_heritage].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).
sections.Scene: Le Chariot (The Chariot) shows a crowned figure standing or seated in a stone canopied vehicle, holding a scepter and wearing armor with epaulettes of two contrasting colors (one blue, one red — representing the opposing forces held in tension). The chariot is drawn by two horses or sphinxes of contrasting colors (one light, one dark). The charioteer does not hold reins but controls the animals through will. In the Conver 1760, the chariot is a massive architectural vehicle with a starry canopy, the contrasting sphinxes or horses below embodying the mastery of opposing forces.
sections.Symbol: The Charioteer in armor between two sphinxes or horses — the will that masters opposing forces without reins, victory as self-mastery in motion.

sections.Iconography: An enthroned woman holds an upright sword and a level balance, eyes open. In this deck she is VIII.
sections.Upright: Balance, fairness, consequence, the exact weighing of each act.
sections.Reversed: Injustice, imbalance, severity, judgment distorted or evaded.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
An enthroned woman, eyes open, holds an **upright sword** in one hand and a **level balance** in the other. In this deck Justice is **VIII** [@web_marseille_wp; @dummett1980]. 

*Sources:* [@dummett1980], [@web_gd_swap], [@web_marseille_wp].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).
sections.Scene: La Justice (Justice) is a seated female figure holding the upright sword in her right hand and the scales in her left — the classical allegorical Justice of the Western legal tradition. In the Conver 1760, Justice is rendered frontally, her throne flanked by decorative columns or curtains, her posture upright and her gaze direct. The Conver places Justice at VIII (before Strength at XI), following the Marseille sequence rather than the Waite-Smith transposition.
sections.Symbol: Justice seated with sword and scales — the classical virtue of equity and measure at position VIII in the Marseille sequence.

sections.Iconography: A cloaked old man walks with a staff, lifting a lantern half-shielded by his cloak.
sections.Upright: Solitude, prudence, the inner light, the patient search carried one step at a time.
sections.Reversed: Isolation, withdrawal that avoids, a light hoarded or refused.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
A cloaked old man walks with a staff, lifting a **lantern half-shielded by his cloak**. Numbered with the additive **VIIII** [@dummett1980; @web_marseille_wp]. 

*Sources:* [@decker1996], [@dummett1980], [@web_marseille_wp].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/tarot-de-marseille-conver.md).
sections.Scene: L'Hermite (The Hermit) is a solitary old man in a cloak, walking with a staff. He holds a lantern (often six-pointed in the Marseille tradition, rendered as a star-shaped form) in his right hand, raised to illuminate the darkness. His long beard and stooped posture suggest age and spiritual withdrawal. In the Conver 1760, the Hermit faces slightly to the left (toward the past or inward), the lantern held out as the light of inner wisdom carried through the dark. He is the figure of contemplative withdrawal from the social world.
sections.Symbol: The Hermit with raised lantern and staff — solitary inner light carried through darkness, contemplative wisdom as the fruit of withdrawal.

illustrations.scene: Wheel of Fortune (La Roue de Fortune)

sections.Iconography: A wheel on a frame turns: animal-eared creatures rise on one side and fall on the other, a sphinx-like figure crowning the top.
sections.Upright: Change, cycles, fortune turning, the moment of luck or reversal.
sections.Reversed: Bad turns, resistance to change, being dragged by the wheel.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Research note:
A wheel on a frame turns: **animal-eared creatures rise on one side and fall on the other**, with a sphinx-like or crowned figure perched at the top [@dummett1980; @web_marseille_wp]. 

*Sources:* [@decker1996], [@dummett1980], [@web_marseille_wp].

sections.Scene: La Roue de Fortune (The Wheel of Fortune) shows a large wheel with figures ascending and descending — the conventional iconography of Fortune's turning derived from the Boethian tradition. In the Conver 1760, the wheel shows a crowned figure at the top (Fortune at her peak), figures ascending on one side, and a falling figure on the other. Two animals (often a jackal-headed figure and a snake or rabbit) flank or ride the wheel, and the wheel structure itself is a prominent mechanical element. The Sphinx or crowned figure at the top presides over the rotation.
sections.Symbol: The Wheel with ascending and descending figures — Fortune's mechanical indifference, the turning that raises and lowers without regard for merit or prayer.

sections.Iconography: A woman in a wide hat calmly parts the jaws of a lion. In this deck Strength is XI.

sections.Reversed: Brute force, domination by appetite, weakness disguised as control.

sections.Scene: La Force (Strength/Fortitude) shows a female figure in a broad-brimmed hat, opening or closing the mouth of a lion with her bare hands. The lion stands or kneels before her, and the woman's posture is calm and confident — the virtue of fortitude as the patient mastery of the animal nature rather than violent conquest. In the Conver 1760, the Force card shows the characteristic Marseille hat (a wide, decorated brim with a figure-eight or lemniscate curve) and the woman's gentle-but-firm hold on the lion's jaws. The figure's expression is serene, suggesting moral strength over brute force.
sections.Symbol: The Woman calmly mastering the lion's jaws — fortitude as patient dominance over the animal nature, gentle strength rather than violent conquest.

sections.Scene: Le Pendu (The Hanged Man) shows a figure suspended by one foot from a wooden gallows — a T-shaped or post-and-beam structure with his hands bound behind his back. The Hanged Man's expression in the Conver 1760 is calm or even serene, suggesting willing suspension rather than punishment. His free leg is bent at the knee in the characteristic crossed-legs posture. Two cut branches on the post suggest the living tree converted to instrument of suspension. The figure wears the colored hose and doublet of the Marseille tradition, rendered in the primary woodblock colors.
sections.Symbol: The Figure suspended by one foot from a gallows — willing suspension and the reversal of worldly values, the view from the inverted perspective.

sections.Iconography: A skeleton with a scythe mows a black ground from which hands, feet, and crowned heads sprout. The card bears its number XIII but no name.

sections.Reversed: Stagnation, fear of change, decay resisted instead of released.

sections.Scene: La Mort (Death) — the only Marseille trump that appears without a title in the Conver 1760 (the space for the name is blank) — shows a skeletal figure wielding a large scythe that cuts through a field of body parts: severed heads, hands, and feet emerging from the ground. The skeleton walks or strides through the scene, the scythe swinging low. The severed body parts include a crowned head (suggesting no one is exempt from Death's harvest) and a female head. The absence of the name 'La Mort' on the card was a common Marseille convention, the superstition being that naming Death would invoke it.
sections.Symbol: The Unnamed skeleton with scythe harvesting body parts — the universal harvester whose name is omitted in the Conver tradition, death cutting through every rank indifferently.
sections.Why XIII has no name:
Trump XIII is the one Marseille trump printed **without its title**, the name-panel left blank. The blank came first and was a **printers' convention** — plausibly a trace of superstition about writing Death's name, but in any case a trade habit of the Marseille woodblock workshops rather than a hidden doctrine [@dummett1980]. The esoteric reading came **afterward**: in 1781 Court de Gébelin reinterpreted the blank as *meaningful* — Death, he wrote, is unnumbered and unnamed "because it belongs to no rank," the great equaliser standing outside all hierarchy. That is a later overlay on an existing convention, not the reason the card was left blank.

By this period the **scythe** has fully displaced the earlier attributes of the oldest Italian decks (such as the archer's bow of the Cary-Yale): Marseille Death is the reaper mowing a field from which crowned heads, hands, and feet sprout — the harvest metaphor that becomes the card's standard image.

sections.Scene: Tempérance (Temperance) shows a winged female figure pouring liquid between two vessels — a large ewer and a cup, the liquid flowing in a thin stream. In the Conver 1760, the figure is a standing angel with large blue and red wings, her robes in the Marseille primary colors, the two vessels at different heights allowing the measured transfer of liquid. The pouring is precise and controlled, the virtue of Temperance expressed as the careful management of flow between two containers.
sections.Symbol: The Winged Angel pouring between two vessels — temperance as measured flow and self-regulation, the precise management of what passes between two containers.

sections.Reversed: Release from bondage, or deeper enslavement: obsession, the chains embraced.

sections.Scene: Le Diable (The Devil) in the Conver 1760 shows a large bat-winged figure on a pedestal or platform, with horns, an animal face, and a torch or flaming club. Below him, two smaller chained figures (a male and a female demon or human) stand bound to the pedestal. The Devil figure has clawed feet, animal legs, a secondary face on his belly, and holds his flaming club aloft. The composition follows the conventional Marseille Devil type: the central figure of infernal authority above the two bound lesser figures, the whole scene rendered in the bold woodblock style.
sections.Symbol: The Devil on a pedestal with two chained figures below — infernal authority over those bound by appetite and illusion, the materialist who does not know he is chained.

sections.Iconography: Lightning knocks the crown from a tower; two figures fall headlong amid coloured spheres. Its name, 'the House of God', is older than 'the Tower'.

sections.Reversed: Disaster resisted, a collapse delayed but coming, clinging to ruins.

sections.Scene: La Maison-Dieu (The House of God / The Tower) in the Conver 1760 shows a tall tower being struck by lightning (a bolt or crown-shaped flash from above), with two figures falling head-first from the battlements. The tower structure is typically rendered with a crown at the top that is struck off by the lightning bolt — the crown being separated from the tower by the force of the strike. Flames or sparks emerge from the windows and top of the tower. The two falling figures, in different-colored clothing, arc away from the tower in symmetrical or asymmetric falls.
sections.Symbol: The Lightning-struck Tower with two falling figures — the crown struck from the top of institutional ambition, the catastrophic blow that descends from outside all human planning.

sections.Scene: L'Étoile (The Star) in the Conver 1760 shows a nude female figure kneeling at the edge of a pool or stream, pouring water from two vessels — one onto the water and one onto the land. Above her a large central star (eight-pointed) is surrounded by seven smaller stars in the night sky. Behind her, a bird (often an ibis) perches in a tree. The woman's posture is one of graceful offering, her body unclothed as a sign of natural or divine revelation. The characteristic Marseille Star uses the eight-pointed large star and seven smaller stars above the pouring figure.
sections.Symbol: The Nude Woman pouring beneath eight-pointed star — celestial guidance poured into the world, the natural revelation that follows catastrophe.

sections.Iconography: Under a dripping face-moon two dogs bay between two towers while a crayfish climbs from a pool.

sections.Scene: La Lune (The Moon) in the Conver 1760 shows a large moon face in the sky (often showing a profile or full face in the disc, surrounded by drops or rays falling toward the earth), while below a pool or stream between two towers is flanked by a lobster or crayfish crawling toward the surface. Two dogs (or a dog and a wolf) bay at the moon from either side. The falling drops or rays create a distinctive shower from the moon to the earth. The scene is night, the dogs on a path between the two towers that frame the distant landscape.
sections.Symbol: The Moon with baying dogs, crayfish, and falling drops — the distorting light of reflected consciousness, the unconscious realm between the two towers of waking and sleeping.

sections.Iconography: Two children stand by a low wall beneath a great rayed face-sun that drips golden tears.

sections.Scene: Le Soleil (The Sun) in the Conver 1760 shows a large sun face radiating straight and wavy alternating rays toward the earth. Below, two nude children (a boy and a girl, or two boys) stand on earth or play together in front of a low stone wall, their hands perhaps joined. Falling drops or swirling forms descend from the sun. The children's nakedness represents innocence and the joyful return to natural simplicity after the trials of the nocturnal cards. The Conver Sun uses the characteristic alternating straight-and-wavy ray pattern around the solar face.
sections.Symbol: The Sun radiating rays over two nude children at a wall — joyful consciousness in the light, innocence and simplicity as the gift of solar illumination.

sections.Scene: Le Jugement (Judgement) in the Conver 1760 shows a large angel with trumpet descending from above, the blast of the horn summoning three figures — a man, a woman, and a child — who rise from a coffin or from the earth below, their arms raised. The angel above is often shown with a sun-disc behind its head and a blue background. The three rising figures form a family unit: the resurrected community called to account at the end of time. The Judgement in the Marseille tradition follows the conventional Christian eschatological iconography.
sections.Symbol: The Angel with trumpet summoning three rising figures — the divine call to account, the resurrection of the community from the death of ordinary time.

sections.Scene: Le Monde (The World) in the Conver 1760 shows a nude female figure dancing or striding within a laurel wreath oval (mandorla), her body in the contrapposto posture of classical dance. She holds two wands (one in each hand) and her lower body is draped with a red sash. The mandorla is surrounded by the four fixed signs of the zodiac at the corners: the lion (Leo, fire), the eagle (Scorpio, water), the man or angel (Aquarius, air), and the bull (Taurus, earth). The World dancer moves within the completed cosmos, the whole of creation summarized in her dance.
sections.Symbol: The Nude Dancer in the laurel mandorla with four zodiacal beings at corners — the integration of all opposites, the completed cosmos as joyful dance.

sections.Iconography: Ace of Coins: a single coin laid out in the woodcut's symmetrical pattern, with floral scrollwork between them. Marseille pips are emblematic, not scenic.
sections.Upright: a fresh start, the suit's energy at its purest and most potent — within money, body, work, and the material world.
sections.Reversed: a false start, energy blocked at the source.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Symbol:
Suit: Earth. The coin (denier) is a disc — circle as wholeness, the solar and lunar both, the completed cycle. In Conver's woodcut the coins bear a cross within a circle: sacred geometry of the four directions held inside totality. Earth element: things that can be weighed, held, built, counted.

Number: The Ace is not "one" in a sequence — it is the seed, the pure potential of the suit before it has differentiated into relationship. In Pythagorean terms the monad precedes number; the Ace precedes the pip series. Everything the suit can become is folded into this single emblem.

A single coin: the material gift undivided, the potential wealth before it enters circulation. Seed-capital of the earth realm.
sections.Scene: The Ace of Coins (As de Deniers) in the Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille shows a single large coin or disc within a decorative floral or foliage surround — the centered pip as the concentrated emblem of the suit. The woodblock printing renders the coin face and its ornamental frame in the bold line style of the Marseille tradition, the suit symbol isolated as the seed of all material potential.

sections.Iconography: Two of Coins: 2 coins laid out in the woodcut's symmetrical pattern, with floral scrollwork between them. Marseille pips are emblematic, not scenic.
sections.Upright: balance, partnership, a choice held in equilibrium — within money, body, work, and the material world.
sections.Reversed: division, imbalance, a partnership strained.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarot de Marseille was made to play *tarot*, a trick-taking card game (the ancestor of today's French Tarot and the central-European Tarock games) — not for divination. The upright and reversed meanings here are a later overlay, added by Etteilla and the occultists from the 1780s onward; they are not part of the deck's original tradition.
sections.Symbol:
Suit: Earth. The coin (denier) is a disc — circle as wholeness, the solar and lunar both, the completed cycle. In Conver's woodcut the coins bear a cross within a circle: sacred geometry of the four directions held inside totality. Earth element: things that can be weighed, held, built, counted.

Number: Two is the first division: unity splits into polarity, relationship, tension. The dyad is the first number that can hold opposition. Two emblems face each other (or orbit each other) — duality as the precondition of exchange, comparison, and choice.

Two coins in dynamic orbit: exchange, barter, the balance of give-and-take. The figure-eight loop sometimes drawn between them in TdM iconography signals infinity within the economic cycle.
sections.Scene: The Two of Coins (Deux de Deniers) in the Conver 1760 shows two large coin pips connected by a figure-eight or S-shaped ribbon or band — the characteristic Marseille two of coins with its connecting band that links the two discs. This distinctive element (absent in many later decks) makes the two of coins a symbol of the material dynamic: two things in connection, balanced and exchanging.

sections.Iconography: Three of Coins: 3 coins laid out in the woodcut's symmetrical pattern, with floral scrollwork between them. Marseille pips are emblematic, not scenic.
sections.Upright: increase, collaboration, early success — within money, body, work, and the material world.
sections.Reversed: delay, growth stalled, effort scattered.

Number: Three resolves the tension of two by adding a third term: thesis, antithesis, synthesis. The triad is the first stable structure, the triangle, the trinity. Three emblems suggest creative synthesis — something new has emerged from the encounter of opposites.

Three coins: the first completed exchange — buyer, seller, and the good itself. Craft manifested; the triangle of skill, material, and client.
sections.Scene: The Three of Coins (Trois de Deniers) in the Conver 1760 shows three coin pips arranged in a triangular pattern — two at the top and one below, or one above and two below — within a floral or geometric decorative frame. The Marseille three-of-coins follows the playing card convention of geometric pip arrangement.

sections.Iconography: Four of Coins: 4 coins laid out in the woodcut's symmetrical pattern, with floral scrollwork between them. Marseille pips are emblematic, not scenic.
sections.Upright: foundation, rest, consolidation, a settled position — within money, body, work, and the material world.
sections.Reversed: stagnation, rigidity, security clung to.

Number: Four is the number of structure: four corners, four elements, four seasons, four directions. The tetrad is the square, stability made visible. Four emblems arranged symmetrically signal consolidation, foundation, the pause after initial movement.

Four coins at the corners of a square: the stable foundation of material security. What has been built holds.
sections.Scene: The Four of Coins (Quatre de Deniers) in the Conver 1760 shows four coin pips in a square arrangement within a decorative frame. The four coins are arranged at the four corners of the card's center field, their geometric regularity reflecting the stable, foundational quality of the number four in the material suit.

sections.Upright: conflict, difficulty, the testing of the suit — within money, body, work, and the material world.
sections.Reversed: the easing of conflict, or loss deepened.

Number: Five disrupts the stability of four: the pentad adds a fifth term that cannot fit the square. In Neoplatonic tradition five is the number of life (quintessence, the fifth element). He wears the parti-colored clothing of a court page in the Marseille tradition — the bold woodblock colors of blue, red, and yellow — with a feathered hat. The Valet is the youngest male court figure, a diligent student of material matters who studies the coin with the focused attention of the practical learner.

The Queen of Coins is a figure of practical generosity and material stewardship.

Number: The Ace is not "one" in a sequence — it is the seed, the pure potential of the suit before it has differentiated into relationship. In Pythagorean terms the monad precedes number; the Ace precedes the pip series. Everything the suit can become is folded into this single emblem.

sections.Symbol:
Suit: Fire. The baton (bâton) is a living staff — Conver's version shows knotted wood, branches not yet cut from the tree, suggesting organic growth and raw vitality. Fire element: the impulse that initiates, the spark before the plan. The batons interweave like vines, dynamic and entangled.

Number: Two is the first division: unity splits into polarity, relationship, tension. The dyad is the first number that can hold opposition. Two emblems face each other (or orbit each other) — duality as the precondition of exchange, comparison, and choice.

Two batons crossed: the first encounter of two wills, a crossing that is both conflict and cooperation. The crossroads of enterprise.
sections.Scene: The Two of Batons (Deux de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows two knotted club pips crossed or arranged in bilateral symmetry — the first division of the suit's creative potential.

sections.Upright: increase, collaboration, early success — within will, work, enterprise, and growth.
sections.Reversed: delay, growth stalled, effort scattered.

Number: Three resolves the tension of two by adding a third term: thesis, antithesis, synthesis. The triad is the first stable structure, the triangle, the trinity. Three emblems suggest creative synthesis — something new has emerged from the encounter of opposites.

Three batons interlaced: the first stable structure of collaborative will. A project has found its partners; growth is underway.
sections.Scene: The Three of Batons (Trois de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows three knotted club pips in a triangular or vertical arrangement — the suit at three, the triangular expansion of creative fire.

sections.Reversed: stagnation, rigidity, security clung to.

Number: Four is the number of structure: four corners, four elements, four seasons, four directions. The tetrad is the square, stability made visible. Four emblems arranged symmetrically signal consolidation, foundation, the pause after initial movement.

Four batons forming a stable frame: the celebration of completed foundation, the gate or threshold of a new domain claimed.
sections.Scene: The Four of Batons (Quatre de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows four knotted club pips in a cross or square arrangement — the stable foundation of the creative suit.

Number: Five disrupts the stability of four: the pentad adds a fifth term that cannot fit the square. In Neoplatonic tradition five is the number of life (quintessence, the fifth element). In TdM pips five is often asymmetrical — one emblem off-centre — signalling instability, challenge, or the vitality that comes from disequilibrium.

Five batons in active disorder: competition as energy, the scramble before order reasserts. Creative conflict among equals.
sections.Scene: The Five of Batons (Cinq de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows five knotted club pips in an asymmetric arrangement — the mid-point disruption of the creative suit.

sections.Reversed: imbalance returning, harmony lost.

Number: Six is harmony restored after the disruption of five: the hexad is the Star of David, two triangles interlocked, the perfect balance of ascending and descending forces. Six emblems in TdM tend toward even, bilateral symmetry — equilibrium, beauty, reciprocity.

Six batons in triumphal arrangement: the victory procession, the will that has succeeded and is acknowledged. Progress crowned.

Number: Seven is the mystical number: seven planets, seven days, seven liberal arts. The heptad cannot be divided evenly into the decad — it stands alone, indivisible, sacred. Seven emblems in TdM often create an asymmetric or pyramidal arrangement, suggesting abundance tipping toward excess, or achievement that has not yet found its resting form.

Seven batons with one baton raised against six: the defender holding ground, will tested by accumulated opposition. Courage under pressure.
sections.Scene: The Seven of Batons (Sept de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows seven knotted club pips in an asymmetric arrangement — the creative suit at its threshold, courage and persistence before the even eight.

Number: Eight is the number of regeneration and power: 2³, the first cubic number, the octave that returns to the root. In Pythagorean number mysticism eight belongs to justice and balance. Eight emblems fill the card almost completely — the suit energy is near its full expression before the density of nine.

Eight batons in parallel flight: rapid movement, messages in motion, energy that has found its vector and moves without obstacle.
sections.Scene: The Eight of Batons (Huit de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows eight knotted club pips in even pairs or rows — the swift doubling of the creative suit, velocity of motion and communication.

sections.Upright: intensity at its height, the suit almost complete — within will, work, enterprise, and growth.
sections.Reversed: strain, overreach, intensity turned against itself.

Number: Nine is the last single digit, the threshold before completion: 3×3, the fullness of the triad squared. Nine emblems crowd the card, suggesting the suit energy at its most concentrated, sometimes overwhelming. The nines carry an intensity that demands resolution — they are the final test before the ten's conclusion.

Nine batons with one upright and eight in row: the will that has endured, scarred but not broken. Perseverance at its limit — one more threshold.
sections.Scene: The Nine of Batons (Neuf de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows nine knotted club pips in the three-by-three arrangement — the near-completion of the creative suit.

sections.Reversed: burden, excess, a cycle that overstays.

Number: Ten is the decad: return to unity at a higher level, the full cycle completed. In Pythagorean cosmology ten contains all numbers (1+2+3+4=10, the tetractys). Ten emblems fill the card densely and symmetrically, signalling completion, the end of a cycle, and the seed of the next.

Ten batons in maximum load: the burden of accumulated responsibility, the full weight of what enterprise has gathered. Completion through carrying.
sections.Scene: The Ten of Batons (Dix de Bâtons) in the Conver 1760 shows ten knotted club pips in the final arrangement of the suit — the complete expression of creative energy at its apex.

Number: The Page (Valet) is the youngest court: the beginner, the student, the messenger. In TdM hierarchy the Valet stands outside the family triangle of King–Queen–Knight, often depicted as smaller or in a position of attendance. The Page carries the suit's quality in its most unformed, receptive state — potential awaiting direction.
sections.Scene: The Valet de Bâtons (Page of Batons) in the Conver 1760 shows a young male figure holding a knotted club upright, his posture alert and forward-facing. He wears the parti-colored court clothing of the Marseille tradition, a young person of creative enthusiasm and eager enterprise who carries the baton suit's emblem with confident, upright clarity.

Number: The Knight (Cavalier) is the court in motion: mounted, directed, carrying the suit's energy outward. Where the King and Queen hold court, the Knight moves through the world. In TdM iconography the horse embodies the instinctual drive harnessed to human purpose — the suit's force acting rather than containing.
sections.Scene: The Cavalier de Bâtons (Knight of Batons) in the Conver 1760 shows a mounted male figure with a knotted club raised or held forward, his horse in a prancing or rearing posture — the dynamic creative rider who carries the suit's fire into active pursuit. He wears the hat and costume of the Marseille court tradition.

Number: The Queen holds the suit from within: she is the receptive, inward expression of its power — not passive but internalising, cultivating, sustaining. In TdM the Queen faces left (inward) while the King often faces right (outward). She governs through depth rather than declaration.
sections.Scene: The Reine de Bâtons (Queen of Batons) in the Conver 1760 shows a crowned female figure enthroned, holding a knotted club upright and facing forward with confident warmth. Her court dress in the Marseille primary colors reflects the high feminine authority of the creative suit — enthusiasm, generosity, and warm creative power.

Number: The King is the outward authority of the suit: he names, judges, commands. In TdM the King is enthroned, facing out or three-quarters — the suit's power made public, institutionalised, accountable. His authority is the consolidation of all the suit's energies into governance.
sections.Scene: The Roi de Bâtons (King of Batons) in the Conver 1760 shows a crowned male figure enthroned, holding a knotted club as his scepter of creative authority and looking forward with the confidence of a visionary leader. His crown and court dress in the Marseille primary colors reflect the apex of masculine authority in the creative suit — visionary enterprise and passionate leadership.

sections.About the Trumps: Conver's 1760 trumps are the images the whole occult era treated as canonical. This is the deck Court de Gébelin theorised over, Etteilla read from, and Lévi and Wirth re-symbolised. Note the order it fixes: the unnumbered Fool, Justice at VIII, Strength at XI, and Death (XIII) left unnamed — all of which the later Golden Dawn would alter.
sections.How to Use: Read the trumps as the standard reference sequence against which every later esoteric deck defined itself.
sections.Scene: The 22 Major Arcana (Atouts or Trumps) of the Nicolas Conver Tarot de Marseille 1760 form the canonical Marseille trump sequence: the Fool (unnumbered), the Bateleur (I) through the World (XXI). This sequence represents the most definitive surviving reference point for the 'classical' Tarot de Marseille iconographic programme — Nicolas Conver's 1760 woodblock printing is the standard to which all scholarly discussions of the Marseille tradition refer. The Conver Majors reflect centuries of accumulated tradition in the French cardmaking center of Marseille.
sections.Symbol: The 22 Conver Majors — the canonical Marseille allegorical sequence, the most-studied reference point for the classical tarot iconographic programme.

sections.About this Suit: The Coins (Deniers) of the Marseille — the suit of Earth. Its pips are emblematic woodcut arrangements rather than the scenic pictures the later Rider-Waite-Smith would invent.
sections.How to Use: Read the suit for money — using the traditional cartomantic meanings rather than scene-reading.
sections.Scene: The coins suit of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille comprises the Ace through Ten pip cards and four court ranks (Valet, Cavalier, Reine, Roi), totaling 14 cards. In the Conver woodblock printing, the coin pips are rendered as round disc emblems in bold Marseille coloring, and the courts are standing or mounted figures in parti-colored court dress. The coins suit of the Marseille tradition uses the geometric pip arrangement inherited from Italian playing cards.
sections.Symbol: The Conver coins suit — 14 cards in the Marseille pip and court tradition, material resources rendered in the canonical 1760 woodblock style.

sections.About this Suit: The Cups (Coupes) of the Marseille — the suit of Water. Its pips are emblematic woodcut arrangements rather than the scenic pictures the later Rider-Waite-Smith would invent.
sections.How to Use: Read the suit for love — using the traditional cartomantic meanings rather than scene-reading.
sections.Scene: The cups suit of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille comprises the Ace through Ten pip cards and four court ranks, totaling 14 cards. The cup pips are rendered as ornate chalices in the Marseille woodblock style, and the courts show the four ranks in parti-colored court dress. The Ace of Cups in the Conver is a particularly elaborate architectural vessel.
sections.Symbol: The Conver cups suit — 14 cards in the Marseille pip and court tradition, emotional depth rendered in the canonical 1760 woodblock style.

sections.About this Suit: The Swords (Épées) of the Marseille — the suit of Air. Its pips are emblematic woodcut arrangements rather than the scenic pictures the later Rider-Waite-Smith would invent.

sections.Scene: The swords suit of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille comprises the Ace through Ten pip cards and four court ranks, totaling 14 cards. The sword pips are rendered as straight blades in the Marseille woodblock style, and the courts show the four ranks in parti-colored armored or court dress. The Cavalier de Bâtons (Knight of Swords) is typically the most dynamic of the mounted court figures.
sections.Symbol: The Conver swords suit — 14 cards in the Marseille pip and court tradition, mental and martial sharpness rendered in the canonical 1760 woodblock style.

sections.Scene: The batons suit of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille comprises the Ace through Ten pip cards and four court ranks, totaling 14 cards. The baton pips are rendered as knotted clubs with characteristic sprouts or knots suggesting living wood — a distinctive Marseille element — and the courts show the four ranks in parti-colored court dress. The knotted organic quality of the Marseille baton distinguishes it from the smooth wands of later occult decks.
sections.Symbol: The Conver batons suit — 14 cards with knotted organic clubs, the creative suit rendered in the canonical 1760 woodblock style.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “minor arcana” (40).
sections.Scene: A keyword grouping for all 56 Minor Arcana cards of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille — the four suits of 14 cards each (Ace through Ten pips plus four courts). The Minor Arcana of the Marseille tradition use the pip-card style: suit symbols arranged in geometric patterns rather than the scenic illustrations that later characterize the Rider-Waite-Smith tradition. This geometric pip tradition reflects the Italian and French playing card heritage of the tarot's minor suits.
sections.Symbol: The Minor Arcana — 56 cards in the Marseille pip tradition, suit symbols in geometric arrangement rather than scenic illustration.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “major arcana” (22).
sections.Scene: A keyword grouping for all 22 Major Arcana cards of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille — the trump sequence from the Fool (0) through the World (XXI). The Conver Major Arcana are the definitive reference point for the classical Marseille iconographic programme, the most-cited and most-studied set of trump images in the scholarly and practitioner literature on the historical tarot.
sections.Symbol: The Major Arcana — 22 allegorical trumps in the canonical Conver 1760 iconographic programme, the reference standard for the Marseille tradition.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “marseille” (22).
sections.Scene: A keyword grouping identifying all items belonging to the Tarot de Marseille tradition as exemplified by Nicolas Conver's 1760 woodblock deck. 'Marseille' as a label covers the family of related French tarot decks produced in the French playing card center of Marseille from the 17th century onward, of which the Conver 1760 is the canonical reference edition. The Marseille tradition is characterized by its pip-card style, its specific trump iconography, and its four-rank courts (Valet, Cavalier, Reine, Roi).
sections.Symbol: The Marseille tradition — the family of French tarot decks culminating in the canonical 1760 Conver, the reference standard for classical tarot iconography.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “court card” (16).
sections.Scene: A keyword grouping for all 16 court cards across the four suits of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille. The Marseille courts use a four-rank system: Valet (Page), Cavalier (Knight), Reine (Queen), and Roi (King). The Conver court cards are rendered in the bold woodblock primary colors of the Marseille tradition, the figures in parti-colored court dress against simple backgrounds.
sections.Symbol: Court cards — the four ranks of court figures in each suit, the Marseille hierarchy of Valet, Cavalier, Reine, and Roi.

sections.Symbol: Air — the mental element associated with the swords suit, swift and cutting like the blade in the Marseille elemental system.

sections.Symbol: Batons — the knotted club suit of creative fire, organic living wood as the emblem of the Marseille tradition's fiery suit.

sections.Symbol: Coins — the circular disc suit of material resources, earthly prosperity in the Marseille pip tradition.

sections.Symbol: Cups — the chalice suit of emotional and spiritual depth, the ornate vessel tradition of the Tarot de Marseille.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “ace” (4).
sections.Scene: A keyword grouping for all four aces across the suits of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille. The aces are the concentrated seeds of each suit — a single coin, a single cup, a single sword, a single knotted club — the undivided potential from which each suit's numerical expansion grows.
sections.Symbol: Aces — the concentrated seeds of each suit in the Conver Marseille, undivided potential before the numerical expansion.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “cavalier” (4).
sections.Scene: A keyword grouping for all four Cavalier (Knight) court figures across the suits of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille. The Cavaliers are mounted male figures bearing each suit's emblem, the active and dynamic second rank of the Marseille court system. Each Cavalier's horse posture reflects the temperament of its suit: the Swords Cavalier charges, the Coins Cavalier walks methodically.
sections.Symbol: Cavaliers — the four mounted knights of the Marseille courts, each suit's active dynamic rank in motion on horseback.

sections.Description: Cross-cutting facets derived from shared keywords — the same card appears under every keyword it carries.
sections.Scene: The keyword axis of the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille organizes the deck's 118 items by thematic grouping — suits (French and English names), elements, pip numbers, court ranks (French and English), and the major/minor arcana distinction. This axis allows navigation across the canonical Marseille deck through the conceptual patterns that structure its 78 cards: the elemental suit system, the numerical pip progression, and the four-rank court hierarchy.
sections.Symbol: The keyword axis — a thematic cross-index of the canonical Conver Marseille, the conceptual map of the most-studied deck in the classical tarot tradition.
